authoritySource: Dr. Ahmed Hesham — The Tutor Book — OET Speaking Comprehensive Rulebook (AI Training Edition). Dentistry profile derived from the medicine rulebook with dental jargon-to-plain mappings appended; the 62 rule bodies are identical because Dr. Hesham's speaking rules are profession-agnostic.

sections.title: Language Rules: Medical Jargon vs. Layman's Language

rules.title: Two-card exam structure
rules.body: The OET Speaking sub-test consists of exactly two role-play cards: Card 1 and Card 2. Each card: 3 minutes preparation + 5 minutes discussion = 8 minutes per card. Total exam duration: approximately 20–22 minutes. Warm-up questions precede the cards and are NOT counted in the final score.

rules.title: Recording-based assessment (audio is the graded artefact)
rules.body: The OET Assessor grades the AUDIO RECORDING after the exam — not the live interaction. Paper-based exam: audio-only recording, no camera. Computer-based exam: the webcam is on for proctoring and to verify paper destruction at the end (see RULE_62), but eye contact, hand gestures, and facial expressions are STILL NOT assessed — only the audio recording is scored.

rules.title: Paper vs computer schedule
rules.body: Paper-based: Speaking on the same day as Listening and Reading. Computer-based: Speaking usually scheduled on a different day.

rules.title: Passing grade
rules.body: Passing grade: Grade B (approximately 350 on the OET scale). Warm-up questions do not count toward the grade.

rules.title: Card tasks are guides, not scripts
rules.body: The tasks listed on the card are NOT obligatory — they are guides. Task order is NOT obligatory — follow the patient's lead. Never treat the card as a lecture script; the conversation must be a ping-pong dialogue.

rules.title: Core language rule — no jargon
rules.body: Never use dental or medical jargon or abbreviations with patients. Replace every technical term with its plain English equivalent. Two approaches: (a) layman's term only, or (b) technical term + ask for feedback + explain.

rules.title: Imaging investigations
rules.body: CT scan → imaging scan. MRI scan → imaging scan. X-ray → X-ray (acceptable as-is). Dental panoramic / OPG → wide dental X-ray. CBCT / cone-beam → 3D dental scan.

rules.title: Injections
rules.body: Local anaesthetic injection → 'a numbing injection in your gum'. Inferior alveolar nerve block → 'a numbing injection to the lower jaw'. IV/IM injection → 'a jab'.

rules.title: Monitoring & investigations
rules.body: Blood pressure (BP) → 'measure the pressure in your blood vessels'. Pulp vitality test → 'a small test to see if the nerve inside your tooth is healthy'. Periodontal probing → 'a small measurement around your gums'.

rules.title: Body parts and dental anatomy
rules.body: Abdomen → tummy. Oesophagus → food pipe. Trachea → windpipe. Gingiva → gum. Maxilla → upper jaw. Mandible → lower jaw. Occlusion → 'the way your teeth bite together'. Dentition → 'your teeth'.

rules.title: Specialists
rules.body: Endodontist → 'root-canal specialist'. Periodontist → 'gum specialist'. Orthodontist → 'specialist who straightens teeth'. Prosthodontist → 'specialist in dentures and crowns'. Oral surgeon → 'mouth surgery specialist'. General principle: [specialty] + 'specialist'.

rules.title: Feedback approach
rules.body: If you use a technical term, immediately ask for feedback: 'I'm sorry for the technical term — have you heard of [condition] before?' Then explain in plain English regardless. The safest approach is to use layman's language directly.
exemplarPhrases: I'm sorry for the technical term — have you heard of {condition} before?

rules.title: Opening — greeting and introduction
rules.body: Begin with a professional greeting. State your name and role, invite the patient to sit. Ask an open-ended opening question.
exemplarPhrases: Hello, Mr Jones. I'm Dr Smith, the dentist looking after you today. Please take a seat. How can I help you?
Hello, nice to see you again. As a reminder, I'm Dr Smith — I treated you for {condition}.

rules.title: Active listening during history
rules.body: Allow the patient to tell their story without interruption. Use active listening signals: 'I see', 'Okay', 'Right', 'Mm-hmm'. Do NOT speak over the patient.

rules.title: Empathy after patient story
rules.body: After the patient finishes, ALWAYS express empathy before moving forward. Acknowledge what the patient has been through (dental pain can be intensely distressing).
exemplarPhrases: I'm really sorry to hear that — toothache like this can be very debilitating. That must have been very difficult for you.

rules.title: Asking permission
rules.body: Always ask the patient's permission before proceeding with more questions or an examination of the mouth.
exemplarPhrases: Would you mind if I asked you some more questions about your symptoms?
Would it be alright if I had a look inside your mouth now?

rules.title: Establishing the diagnosis
rules.body: Check the patient's prior knowledge before delivering a diagnosis. Present in plain language — never cause unnecessary alarm.
exemplarPhrases: Based on what you've told me and what I can see, it looks like you have a condition called {condition} — have you heard of it before?

rules.title: Feedback after diagnosis
rules.body: After explaining, ALWAYS take feedback from the patient. This is a core OET communication requirement.
exemplarPhrases: Does that make sense to you?
Is there anything you'd like me to clarify?

rules.title: Explaining causes
rules.body: Explain the cause(s) in plain language. Link known risk factors from the patient's history to the condition (e.g. sugar intake, brushing habits, smoking).

rules.title: Recap / summarising
rules.body: Briefly recap everything discussed at the end of the card. Keep it to approximately 30 seconds.
exemplarPhrases: So, to recap: you have {condition}, we've talked about {changes}, and I've arranged {treatment}. Any questions?

rules.title: Closing the card
rules.body: End with a standard, professional closing phrase. Offer ongoing support and wish the patient well.
exemplarPhrases: I wish you all the best. Please don't hesitate to come back if you have any concerns.

rules.title: Ping-pong conversation rule
rules.body: The conversation MUST be a two-way dialogue — never a monologue. Speak, then let the patient respond. Continuous speaking without patient input is penalised.

rules.title: Weight — ask sensitively
rules.body: NEVER ask 'What is your weight?' directly — blunt and disrespectful. Approach sensitively. Asking weight directly is a communication failure in OET.
exemplarPhrases: Have you ever had any concerns about your weight?
Would you mind if I noted your height and weight to check your BMI?

rules.title: Exercise — ask and advise
rules.body: Ask about exercise habits before giving advice. Suggest specific, achievable targets.
exemplarPhrases: Do you do any regular exercise at the moment?
Even a 30-minute walk each day can make a big difference.

rules.title: Diet — ask and advise
rules.body: Ask about dietary habits using an open question. Advise on specific dietary changes relevant to the patient's condition — for dentistry, particularly sugar intake and acidic drinks.
exemplarPhrases: How would you describe your diet? Do you have many sugary or fizzy drinks?
I'd strongly advise cutting down on sugary snacks between meals.

rules.title: Smoking — initial assessment
rules.body: Always ask whether the patient smokes before giving advice. Smoking is a major risk factor for gum disease, oral cancer, and implant failure. If yes, explore: what, how many per day, how long.
exemplarPhrases: Do you smoke at all?
How many cigarettes do you smoke per day?

rules.title: Smoking — graduated negotiation
rules.body: Use graduated negotiation — always start from the strongest recommendation. Step 1 (Black Area): Recommend complete cessation. Step 2 (if refused): Offer NRT alternatives. Step 3 (Grey Zone): Negotiate a reduction. NEVER start with Step 3 — you lose all negotiating leverage.

rules.title: Smoking — empathy
rules.body: Acknowledge that quitting is genuinely difficult. Show empathy while maintaining your clinical recommendation.
exemplarPhrases: I understand it's not easy, and I know many people find it very challenging to quit.

rules.title: Smoking — mention cessation support
rules.body: Always mention the smoking cessation clinic or local stop-smoking service as a concrete resource.
exemplarPhrases: We have a smoking cessation clinic locally that could provide you with great support.

rules.title: Alcohol — assessment and advice
rules.body: Ask about frequency and quantity (units per week). For dentistry, link alcohol to oral cancer risk where relevant. Options are cessation or reduction; no 'alternatives' for alcohol. Refer to an alcohol support service if appropriate.

rules.title: Patient autonomy
rules.body: Always respect patient autonomy — you advise, not command.
exemplarPhrases: No one can force you to do anything. I can only advise you — the decision is entirely yours.

rules.title: Do not over-diagnose from one reading
rules.body: Do NOT make a definitive diagnosis from a single finding. Inform calmly and arrange appropriate investigations. Saying 'You definitely have oral cancer' from one inspection is clinically wrong and costs marks.
exemplarPhrases: What I'm seeing is a little concerning, so I'd like to arrange some further tests. Have you noticed this changing over time?

rules.title: Reassurance after results
rules.body: When results are not serious, always reassure the patient. Acknowledge concerns and reframe positively.

rules.title: Specific dietary recommendations
rules.body: Advise against: sugary snacks between meals, acidic drinks (fizzy/citrus), fast food. Advise for: water, plain milk, vegetables and whole grains.

rules.title: Identifying a second-visit card
rules.body: Look for: 'coming back for...', 'returning for...', 'follow-up', 'review of...', 'post-extraction review'. 'Your patient...' alone does NOT confirm a second visit.

rules.title: Second-visit greeting
rules.body: Use a modified greeting acknowledging the previous encounter. A brief reminder of who you are is sufficient.
exemplarPhrases: Hello, it's lovely to see you again, Mr Jones.
Hello, good to have you back. As a reminder, I'm Dr Smith — I treated you for {condition}.

rules.title: Test result cards
rules.body: Most common second-visit card: patient returns to hear results (e.g. biopsy, panoramic X-ray). Before revealing results, check the support system.

rules.title: Review of treatment
rules.body: Ask about: how the patient is healing, side effects, pain control, compliance with after-care instructions, and concerns. Adjust the plan based on feedback.

rules.title: Review of disease
rules.body: Check on symptom changes, new symptoms, and medication or oral-hygiene adherence. Provide updated management plans as needed.

rules.title: Tone of voice
rules.body: Your tone must be soft, low, and genuinely empathetic. Since there is no video assessment, tone of voice is the PRIMARY communication tool. Speaking about a serious diagnosis (e.g. suspected oral cancer) in a neutral or upbeat tone will cause very poor marks.

rules.title: Step 1 — support system
rules.body: Before delivering any results, ask if the patient would like someone with them.
exemplarPhrases: Before we discuss your results, is there anyone you'd like to have here with you?

rules.title: Step 2 — warning shots
rules.body: Do NOT deliver a serious diagnosis suddenly. Build up gradually with warning shots.
exemplarPhrases: I'm afraid the results are not quite what we had hoped for.
The biopsy is showing something more serious than we expected...

rules.title: Step 3 — deliver diagnosis
rules.body: After warning shots, state the diagnosis directly but gently. Use plain language.
exemplarPhrases: I'm very sorry to tell you — the results are showing signs of oral cancer.

rules.title: Step 4 — silence 3–4 seconds
rules.body: After stating the diagnosis, STOP SPEAKING for 3–4 seconds. Allow the patient to absorb the shock. Continuing to speak immediately after delivering serious news is one of the most common exam mistakes.

rules.title: Step 5 — respond to emotion
rules.body: After the silence, acknowledge whatever reaction the patient has. Never dismiss or minimise emotions.
exemplarPhrases: I'm so sorry. I know this is a lot to take in. Please take all the time you need.

rules.title: Step 6 — hope + next steps
rules.body: After acknowledging emotions, provide forward-looking information. Mention early stage if applicable.
exemplarPhrases: I want you to know that we caught this at an early stage, and there are effective treatment options available.

rules.title: Step 7 — end with support system
rules.body: Return to the support system theme at the end.
exemplarPhrases: Is there someone who can be with you today?
I'm here for you, and my number is available whenever you need anything.

rules.title: Proportionality
rules.body: Full BBN protocol: serious diagnoses such as oral cancer only. Minor bad news (loose tooth, mild gum disease): simple acknowledgement and reassurance only.

rules.title: 3-minute prep strategy
rules.body: Read the entire card start to finish first. Underline key words. Write a brief 1–2 word label next to each task. During the 5-minute discussion, glance at notes — don't re-read the full card.

rules.title: Warm-up question prep
rules.body: Typical warm-ups: your name, where you work, why you're taking OET, dental specialty/interest, career goals. Do NOT memorise scripts — adapt naturally. Speak confidently.

rules.title: Reduce hesitation
rules.body: Avoid filler words: 'umm', 'err', 'so...', 'like...'. Practice compresses the translation process until hesitation disappears.

rules.title: Quality over quantity
rules.body: You do NOT need to complete all tasks to pass. A fluent, empathetic 3-task conversation scores better than a rushed 5-task performance. If you run out of time, close professionally.

rules.title: Use flexible phrases
rules.body: Internalise ready-to-use phrases for: greetings, empathy, permission, diagnosis, advice, closure. Adapt naturally.

rules.title: Where to look
rules.body: Since eye contact is NOT assessed: keep eyes on the card throughout. Reduces cognitive load and helps fluency.

rules.title: Differential diagnosis formats
rules.body: Two acceptable formats: 'Possible diagnosis: X or Y' OR 'Differential diagnosis of X and Y'. Do NOT write both formats together.

rules.title: 9 scoreable criteria — scored separately
rules.body: The OET Speaking recording is scored against NINE criteria, not six. Linguistic criteria (4, each 0–6): Intelligibility, Fluency, Appropriateness of Language, Resources of Grammar & Expression. Clinical Communication criteria (5, each 0–3 — descriptors: Adept / Competent / Partially effective / Ineffective): Relationship Building; Understanding & Incorporating the Patient's Perspective; Providing Structure; Information Gathering; Information Giving. Grouping Clinical Communication into a single aggregate score is WRONG — every feedback item must cite one of the nine criterion codes AND a rule ID.

rules.title: Interlocutor does NOT grade
rules.body: The interlocutor is an ACTOR: they facilitate the role play, read warm-up questions, and play the patient/carer. The interlocutor NEVER assesses or scores the candidate. Any feedback that treats the interlocutor as the examiner is architecturally wrong.

rules.title: OET Assessor grades the recording
rules.body: A separate OET Assessor listens to the audio recording AFTER the exam and scores it against all 9 criteria. The platform's AI evaluation pipeline must therefore evaluate the recording post-hoc, not live interaction dynamics that are not audible on the recording.

rules.title: Computer-based Speaking is always at home
rules.body: In the computer-based OET, Speaking is always taken at home — never at a test centre. The device check and mic check flow must enforce or clearly inform the candidate of the full environment requirement list (RULE_60) before the exam can start.

rules.title: Computer-based environment requirements
rules.body: Location: at home only. Room: indoors, well-lit, solid walls (no glass panels or transparent partitions). Other people: candidate must be completely alone — if anyone enters the room, the exam is stopped. Monitors: single monitor only. Webcam: wired or built-in webcam that can move; Bluetooth webcam NOT permitted. Microphone & speakers: built-in or USB plug-in only; Bluetooth NOT allowed; NO headsets during Speaking. Power: device plugged directly into power; no docking station; no battery-only. VPN / Virtual machines: fully disabled before the exam starts. Extra screens: all other screens (TV, second monitor) unplugged.

rules.title: Paper + pen permitted; on-screen annotation is NOT
rules.body: Computer-based Speaking: candidates CANNOT highlight or annotate the role-play card on screen. However they ARE permitted ONE blank piece of paper and a pen to take notes during preparation time and during the role play. Paper-based Speaking: candidates may annotate the card with pencil (no separate paper needed).

rules.title: Destroy paper at end of computer-based exam
rules.body: At the end of the computer-based Speaking exam, the candidate MUST tear or cut the blank paper in front of the camera to confirm nothing is removed from the test environment. The preparation screen must explicitly inform the candidate of this before the exam starts.

laymanGlossary.example: We'll do an imaging scan to see the bone around your tooth
We'll do an imaging scan
A wide dental X-ray that shows all your teeth
A wide dental X-ray of your whole mouth
A 3D dental scan to plan the implant
A 3D dental scan

laymanGlossary.plain: small X-ray between your back teeth
laymanGlossary.example: A small X-ray between your back teeth to check for hidden decay

laymanGlossary.plain: test to see if the nerve inside your tooth is healthy
laymanGlossary.example: A small test to see if the nerve inside your tooth is healthy
A tiny measurement around your gums to check their health
A numbing injection so you won't feel the procedure

laymanGlossary.plain: numbing injection to the lower jaw
laymanGlossary.example: A numbing injection to your lower jaw
The gum around your tooth is a little swollen
Early gum inflammation that can be reversed with better cleaning
Advanced gum disease affecting the bone around your teeth
The pain is coming from your upper jaw
A wisdom tooth in your lower jaw

laymanGlossary.plain: the way your teeth bite together
laymanGlossary.example: I'd like to check the way your teeth bite together
An uneven bite that may be causing your jaw pain
A small area of tooth decay on this back tooth
We'd place a small white filling
Taking the tooth out is the safest option
Root-canal treatment to save the tooth
A denture or false tooth to fill the gap
A cap on the tooth to protect it
Tooth grinding, often at night
A jaw-joint problem causing the clicking
Persistent bad breath
Your reading is on the higher side

consultationStages.name: greeting
consultationStages.example: Hello, I'm Dr X. Please take a seat.

consultationStages.name: opening
consultationStages.example: How can I help you today?

consultationStages.name: empathy
consultationStages.example: That must have been very painful.

consultationStages.name: permission
consultationStages.example: Would you mind if I asked some questions?

consultationStages.name: questions
consultationStages.example: Can you tell me more about the pain?

consultationStages.name: diagnosis
consultationStages.example: It looks like you have... Have you heard of this?

consultationStages.name: lifestyle
consultationStages.example: I'd strongly advise you to cut down on sugary snacks...

consultationStages.name: treatment
consultationStages.example: I'd like to arrange a filling. How does that sound?

consultationStages.name: reassurance
consultationStages.example: I want to reassure you we can manage this well.

consultationStages.name: recap
consultationStages.example: So to recap what we've discussed...

consultationStages.name: closure
consultationStages.example: Please don't hesitate to come back anytime.

breakingBadNewsProtocol.name: support_system
breakingBadNewsProtocol.what: Ask if the patient would like someone with them.

breakingBadNewsProtocol.name: deliver
breakingBadNewsProtocol.what: State diagnosis directly but gently in plain language.

smokingLadder.name: nrt
smokingLadder.what: Offer NRT alternatives if cessation refused.

smokingLadder.name: reduction
smokingLadder.what: Negotiate a reduction (Grey Zone). NEVER start here.
